1: The sin-cycle formula: wayyôsipû bənê Yiśrāʾēl laʿăśôt hāraʿ bəʿênê YHWH — “the Israelites again did evil in the sight of the LORD.” Forty years of Philistine oppression is the longest in Judges (cf. Midian 7 years, Ammon 18 years). The Philistines — sea-peoples settled on the Coastal Plain — represent a qualitatively different and more persistent threat than the Transjordanian enemies of earlier chapters.
2: ʿăqārāh (H6135, barren/sterile) — Manoah’s wife joins the barren-matriarch sequence: Sarah (Genesis 11:30), Rebekah (Genesis 25:21), Rachel (Genesis 29:31), Hannah (1 Samuel 1:2), Elizabeth (Luke 1:7). Each barrenness is a narrative marker for divine intervention; the child born from barrenness carries a special commission. wəhîʾ lōʾ yālādāh (she had borne no children) confirms the condition, not merely a state of delay.
3: malʾak YHWH (H4397 + H3068, Angel of the LORD) — the definite article identifies this as the recurring divine messenger of the Exodus and pre-Sinai narratives (Gen 16, 22; Exod 3; Judg 2, 6). The angel’s appearance to the woman first — before Manoah receives any word — is unusual; she receives the annunciation directly. wəhinnāḵ hārāh (and you shall conceive) uses the participial construction of immediate future, identical to Isaiah 7:14 LXX.
4: The dietary prohibitions are addressed to the mother as preparation: haššāmərî nāʾ wəʾal-tištî yayin wəšēḵār — “be careful, drink no wine or strong drink.” šēḵār (H7941, strong drink) is any fermented beverage other than wine. The Nazirite regulations will bind the child from the womb; the mother’s observance during pregnancy extends the Nazirite restrictions to gestation.
5: nāzîr ʾĕlōhîm (H5139, consecrated one of God) — Nazirite, from nāzar (to separate, consecrate). Unlike the voluntary Nazirite vow of Num 6 (which is temporary), Samson’s consecration is lifelong and prenatal — imposed before birth. hûʾ yāḥēl ləhôšîaʿ ʾet-Yiśrāʾēl — “he will begin to save Israel.” yāḥēl (H2490, begin) signals incomplete deliverance; Samson’s salvation is inaugural, not final. hôšîaʿ (H3467) is the verbal root of Joshua (yəhôšûaʿ) and Jesus (Iēsous).
6: The woman’s report to Manoah: ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm (man of God, H376 + H430) — the conventional prophetic title; she does not yet recognize him as an angel. mōrāʾ məʾōd (very awesome/terrifying) — she perceives the visitor’s superhuman nature without identifying it. wəlōʾ šəʾaltîhû ʾayyeh-miZZeh — “I did not ask him where he was from” — the convention in theophany encounters is that the divine visitor’s origin is not to be interrogated.
7: The woman rehearses the message: the angel’s prohibition on wine and unclean food, the Nazirite status from birth. The repetition in Judges 13:4-5 and 7 is a narrative device that emphasizes the requirements and confirms the woman’s faithful transmission to her husband.
8: bî ʾădōnāy — “O Lord.” Manoah’s prayer addresses YHWH as ʾădōnāy (H136, the Lord) — the reverent substitute for the divine name. His request is pastoral rather than skeptical: yəlammēdēnû mah-naʿăśeh lannnaʿar hayyûllād — “teach us what to do for the child who will be born.” He wants instruction for raising the commissioned child, not proof of the message.
9: wayyišmaʿ hāʾĕlōhîm bəqôl Mānôaḥ — “God listened to the voice of Manoah.” The Hiphil šāmaʿ here is divine attentiveness: prayer was heard and acted on. The angel returns, but again ʾel-hāʾiššāh (to the woman) — while she sits in the field, without Manoah present. The woman remains the primary recipient of the divine communication.
10: The woman acts with urgency (wattəmaher, she hurried) to bring Manoah. Her role is mediating — she receives and transmits the divine encounter to her husband. The narrative structure consistently gives her perceptive priority over Manoah, who will need guidance where she understood intuitively.
11: Manoah’s question: hattāh hāʾîš ʾăšer-dibbartā ʾel-hāʾiššāh — “Are you the man who spoke to this woman?” He addresses the angel as a man — not wrong at this point, since the angel appears in human form. The angel’s reply (ʾănî, I am) is laconic; he does not volunteer his identity.
12: mišpaṭ hannaʿar — “the rule for the child.” mišpaṭ (H4941, judgment/ordinance/custom) frames child-rearing as covenantally ordered; Manoah asks for the divinely appointed way of life for the consecrated child. This is a more theologically sophisticated question than simple curiosity.
13: The angel refers Manoah back to what the woman was already told — confirming the authority of her reception of the message. The doubling of instruction (Judges 13:4-5 to the woman; v13 back to the woman) structurally underscores that she is the primary recipient.
14: The Nazirite requirements restated comprehensively: nothing from the vine, no wine or strong drink, nothing unclean — all three Num 6 food restrictions applied to the mother during pregnancy. The totality of the prenatal Nazirite framework is emphasized; nothing of the sacred separation is to be compromised.
15: Manoah’s hospitality: nəʿaṣṣərāh nāʾ ʾôtāḵā — “let us detain you.” The verb ʿāṣar (H6113, to detain/hold back) is the same used when Abraham urged the three visitors to stay (Genesis 18:3-5). The offer of a kid for the meal recalls Abraham’s hospitality; Manoah does not yet know he is hosting the divine.
16: The angel refuses the meal but redirects: ʾim-taʿăśeh ʿôlāh laYHWH taʿălennāh — “if you offer a burnt offering, offer it to YHWH.” This refusal distinguishes the angel from a human guest who would eat, and from a pagan deity who would require a ritual meal; the burnt offering to YHWH is the appropriate form of worship. kî lōʾ yādaʿ Mānôaḥ — the narrator explains: Manoah still does not know he is speaking to the Angel of the LORD.
17: Manoah asks the angel’s name to know whom to honor when the promise is fulfilled: ləḵabbdəḵā — to honor you. The motive is appropriate (honoring one’s benefactor) but the angel’s response reveals that a name would be inadequate to contain his identity.
18: lāmmāh zeh tišʾal lišmî wəhûʾ-pelîʾî — “Why do you ask my name, seeing it is wonderful/beyond understanding?” pālîʾ / pelîʾî (H6383) derives from pālāʾ (H6381, to be wonderful, to be beyond ordinary comprehension). The same root appears in the messianic title of Isaiah 9:6: peleʾ yôʿēṣ (Wonderful Counselor). The angel’s name-refusal is itself a theophanic disclosure: his name is beyond human categories of understanding.
19: Manoah takes the kid and grain offering and offers them on the rock to YHWH — the rock as improvised altar recalls Gideon’s theophany (Judges 6:20-21). hammalpîʾ laʿăśôt — “the one who works wonders” — the participial phrase identifies YHWH/the angel as the subject of miraculous action during the sacrifice. wayyapleʾ (and he worked wondrously) confirms the theophany.
20: The fire theophany: baaʿălôt hallahav mēʿal hammizbaḥ — “as the flame went up from the altar.” The angel of the LORD ascended in the flame — echoing Elijah’s fire-descent from heaven (1 Kgs 18) and the pillar of fire in the Exodus. Manoah and his wife fell on their faces; prostration is the appropriate response to divine presence.
21: wəlōʾ-yāsap malʾak YHWH lĕhērāʾôt — “the Angel of the LORD did not appear again.” The cessation of appearances functions as interpretive closure: the angel’s work is complete. ʾāz yādaʿ Mānôaḥ kî-malʾak YHWH hûʾ — “then Manoah realized that he had been the Angel of the LORD.” The recognition arrives after the encounter ends, not during it — a recurring feature of theophany narratives (Genesis 32:30; Luke 24:31).
22: mût nāmût — emphatic (absolute infinitive + finite): “we will certainly die.” Manoah’s fear is theologically grounded: the tradition that seeing God brings death (Exodus 33:20; Judges 6:22). The phrase ʾĕlōhîm rāʾînû — “we have seen God” — confirms that the Angel of the LORD’s identity equates with seeing YHWH himself; the narrative does not distinguish the angel from God.
23: The wife’s response is a model of theological reasoning under fear: three parallel conditionals — if YHWH had wanted to kill us, he would not have (1) accepted the burnt offering, (2) shown us these things, (3) announced the coming child. Her logic moves from divine action to divine intention; what YHWH has already done is evidence of what he intends. The narrator gives the wife the final word in the theological debate between the two, a pattern of her superior perception throughout Judges 13.
24: watteqrāʾ ʾet-šəmô Šimšôn — “she named him Samson.” It is the mother who names the child — she received the annunciation, and she claims the naming. Šimšôn may derive from šemeš (H8121, sun), appropriate for a Danite hero from the Sorek valley near Bethshemesh (house of the sun). wayyiḡdal hannaʿar wayyəḇāreḵēhû YHWH — the divine blessing at birth is the same formula used for Samson as for Samuel (1 Samuel 3:19) and John the Baptist (Luke 1:80).
25: wattāḥel rûaḥ YHWH lipʿămô — “the Spirit of the LORD began to stir him.” pāʿam (H6470, to beat/push/stir) describes the intermittent, recurrent movement of the Spirit; it is not a continuous indwelling but a periodic stirring — different from the Spirit coming upon a judge for a single event. bəmaḥănēh-dān (in the camp of Dan) between Zorah and Eshtaol — the encampment where the Spirit begins to move becomes the geographic theater for Samson’s coming exploits.
